:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Parole is available to certain inmates who are serving 6-month or longer sentences and have served more than one-fourth of their aggregate sentence. However, an inmate not serving a sentence for a crime of violence or for certain drug crimes may be released on parole at any time to undergo drug or alcohol treatment, mental health treatment, or to participate in a residential program in the best interest of an expected or newborn child. If serving an aggregate sentence where there is a period of no parole eligibility related to one of the sentences, a parolee can be released when they have completed the greater of one-fourth of the aggregate sentence or the period of no parole eligibility. For example, if a person is sentenced to 20 years with standard parole eligibility and another 10 year sentence with parole eligibility after 4 years, they will have to wait five years (20 years * 1/4 = 5 years) to be eligible for release.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 895 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 6% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 68% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 99% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 35-44 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 31% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
